content.assessment: This proposal explicitly targets structural drivers (zoning, choice, legal exemptions) distinct from the tracking mechanism itself, which is consistent with the lens's emphasis that whole-system segregation has multiple independent causal channels requiring coordinated, not singular, correction. However, by design it leaves the tracking funnel and SES-linked admissions advantage untouched, so it addresses only one strand of a multi-strand problem.
opportunities: Directly complements structural-age reforms (T2) or admissions reforms (T3), and the proposal itself acknowledges this by referencing coordination with T4-style levers in other proposals' mechanisms
strengths: Recognizes that segregation has channels independent of tracking age, aligning with the whole-system principle that isolated interventions targeting only one channel (e.g., age or admissions) will not resolve segregation attributable to other channels
Precedent exists that administrative/structural levers of this kind produced at least temporary measurable reduction in segregation (F-live-portuguese_reform-06)
threats: Because segregation is a whole-system property (highest in EU, F-live-portuguese_reform-03), a zoning-only lever risks being overwhelmed by the larger structural funnel and SES-selection effects it does not touch, reproducing the historical stagnation pattern
weaknesses: Same precedent shows these gains stagnated, indicating the equity criterion may only be partially and temporarily satisfied absent complementary quality-levelling or structural age reform
Does not address the SES-linked selection advantage documented independent of geography (F-live-portuguese_reform-02), leaving a core equity mechanism outside its scope
